speaker: interviewer
text: OK, we're going to pick up by the grave site.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. When there wasn't a grave, they just put a few shovels of dirt. And they left. And it-- at daytime, it was done. I laid there, and after a while, I opened my eyes. And I looked around where I am-- where I'm laying-- and I saw all those people. Everybody dead, black and blue from the wounds. [CRYING] Blood was [SOBBING] [INAUDIBLE] [CRYING]

speaker: interviewer
text: So I sat thinking, what shall I do? I'm going to run. And I ran out of-- because I was on top then. So I ran out, and I was starting to walk, not knowing nothing-- where I am, and what I'm doing-- but I shall run. But I wanted to run out of the forest. I was running and running until I came out of the forest.

speaker: interviewee
text: And there was a field. So I was looking for where is high grasses, like there was a field with corn-- they call it in Europe-- like, for rye-- for rye bread, you know. And this comes so high.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I went in there in this, in the middle of this field and sat down. And early in the morning, so it's so wet, the grass.

speaker: interviewee
text: [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] I was starting to think what to do, where I shall go, and what I shall do. [CRYING] My hair was all in clumps, bloody from-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] until I washed a little bit. And then a whole day I was sitting there, the whole-- the other night. And the next day, my hair got a little dry. I was full of blood. I was doing like this to release the blood. [CRYING] I ran away like in three dresses, so I tore out a little piece and made a kerchief for myself to cover my hair from the blood. [CRYING] And I washed up a little my face. I don't know, I didn't have a mirror, nothing, but with the water from the grass. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And then I started walking the next day out there. And I didn't know where to go, but I wanted to go to a village, [NON-ENGLISH] which I knew they were nice people there. There, maybe, I'll be able to hide some there. My father used to do a lot of business with those people. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: So, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] instead, I didn't know which way to go. And I was walking in a different direction. And as I was walking by the field, there were people working in the fields. So one lady ran out and said to me, I know who you are-- meaning that I'm a Jew-- and where do you want to go? Don't go in this way, because this is a very bad village. [SOBBING] If you will go there, you'll be killed right away. The name was [NON-ENLIGH]. That was the name of the woman?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, of-- I didn't know the name of the woman-- She didn't tell me-- but the village I was approaching. So I went to-- she told me. I told her that I want to go to [NON-ENLIGH], and she directed me how I shall go. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] And I start walking and walking there until I came. Not far from this, it was a little forest. I stopped there. And I was thinking, oh, it was already late in the afternoon. I didn't know. Maybe, in the evening I'll go, but I didn't go further. I was waiting there, not knowing where to turn, what I shall do. I never been there. [CRYING]

speaker: interviewer
text: So I-- I walked out of the-- of the forest. And the next day, I went to this village. It wasn't far already.

speaker: interviewee
text: [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] On-- on the way, I stopped in a-- there was a little house, like a room, and a garage-- whatever it was. I went through there. And I figured maybe somebody will let me in there. But nobody let me there. Then, I went back, again, to the forest there, not near the village. It was a little one. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And I figured I'll go to somebody else. I went in to another farmer, and they said they cannot keep me. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: So I went, and I was afraid not to-- this was already [INAUDIBLE]-- I was afraid even to go somewhere, to do-- I have to go back [INAUDIBLE], because running out from-- from when she directed me where to go, I was already so tired. And a few days not eating nothing, and in such agony, that I went in on the field and sat down there. And I fell asleep.

speaker: interviewee
text: In the daytime, there came out men working there. But I didn't hear nobody coming. I didn't see nobody. And I slept. And all of a sudden, when I woke up, I opened my eyes. I see a man standing. So I got so scared. I don't know why. I didn't have nothing what to do, where to turn, what. But the willpower to live was so strong that I really don't know for what I was so scared about. And he said to me, don't be afraid. [SOBBING] I will bring out tonight some water for you that you shall wash up. I probably was all bloody still, even when I washed myself with the water from the grass. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And I'll bring you some food. And you'll wash up. And we'll see what we'll do.

speaker: interviewer
text: So as he left me, I was-- I don't know-- I was scared, and I ran away from there. I didn't-- I went, maybe, I don't know, a two-- a block or two further in the fields. And again, where shall I go? When the night came, I was sleeping there. [CRYING] and not knowing nothing, where to turn, what to do.

speaker: interviewee
text: And they really came out-- he and his son-- and brought out a big pot with two handles with water [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] that I shall wash up. And then they came in their field. I wasn't there.

speaker: interviewer
text: You weren't there anymore?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I went away, I told you before. And because I was afraid, I didn't trust him.

speaker: interviewer
text: So the next day, they-- this man, again, came out there. And he was walking in the fields to look if he can find me. And he came, and he found me there, further than where I was hiding. So he said, why did you-- why weren't you there where I left you? We came out, and we brought you some water and something to eat. Why did you run away?

speaker: interviewee
text: And I said, I didn't trust you. I was afraid. I don't know why I wanted so to live. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: So he said, now don't go nowhere. Tonight, we'll come again, because in the daytime he couldn't bring this. And at night he came again. And I really didn't have nowhere to turn, so I accepted. I was there. Where shall I go? What will I do?

speaker: interviewee
text: [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] They brought out water. And I washed up. And they brought me a bowl of soup [CRYING] and a piece of bread, milk. [CRYING] And he said, I will come out. Remain here in this field. I will come out every day, and I will bring you something to eat. And maybe we will find some kind of a place for you. I didn't know those people, but people knew my father. They probably knew maybe me, too. And I don't know. But my father they knew because he was a businessman. So they knew-- And he said, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I will bring you every night something here. And I didn't have nowhere left to go.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I was there in this field.

speaker: interviewee
text: [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And he used to bring me, though, every day some food, and-- until the corns, they had to-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I don't know how do you say, they take it off, and they thresh this, this everything.

speaker: interviewer
text: So there were only, like, potatoes. Potatoes grow low, so you cannot hide. So, but I was-- but they so thick that I used to hide sometimes there.

speaker: interviewee
text: And he said that now it's not so danger-- I used to run in the daytime to the forest there and hide, like, under trees, sitting or somewhere when I hear somebody's coming. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And for a few months he was bringing me some food. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And I never trust him. This son of his and he used to say, I want to save her Jewish life, and I want to save a Jewish girl. And that's all. But I didn't trust them. Every day, when they brought out the food, I was thinking, he's going to kill me. He'll let me eat, and then he'll kill me. Every day I was under constant pressure that I'll [SOBBING] be killed.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you were all alone this whole time?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: So how long did this go on for?

speaker: interviewee
text: Until it started getting cold. And I went-- there was, in another side by the forest there, a little house where a woman with her daughter used to live. And, a poor lady, she had the-- I used to come there. It was like a stack. And it was four poles. And there was bundles make of straw, corns, and all those things.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I used to pull out-- because it was already cold-- a bundle of straw and crawl in there. And I just had a place to sit like this, in a clump. Because I didn't want that somebody shall know, I pulled it out. I crawled in, and pulled the straw back, that it shouldn't see that somebody is there. And I used to go, so, for a few weeks. And one day, they came. And they were-- this was corn-- they were threshing it, or how you say, to take out from the straw those little wheats, or what, that they make flour of it? So they-- [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] a man was taking out this with a-- with a stick. They banged like this, and do this. So in this night, just, I decided not to go back, because early in the morning I used to run back to the forest. And at night when it got dark, I used to run back to the stack and hide there because it was already so cold.

speaker: interviewee
text: [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And this day, when they came to do this, they start to take out the bundles of the straw. But I didn't have already place to run out. I was just afraid that they discover me. And I-- So it was dark there, but I saw already above me lights. And this man who was doing it, he saw, like, a head there too. And when he-- because I was told this later, he was-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] when he saw that somebody sitting there, he put back the-- the bundles of straw. And he stopped the doing to do this. And he told this lady that somebody's hiding there. And she said, well, I don't know. If it will be a girl-- so, a lady or a girl-- so I'll-- I'll take her into the house. And if it's a man, so I'm afraid, because he shouldn't do something to me instead, because sometimes they were afraid they'll-- for them, I don't know why. [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] And I was waiting until there is no lights. So I used to crawl in there and hide again. And I hear somebody's coming there, and say-- and she was watching by the window who it is. And she saw it's a girl, so she said-- she came out and said, come-- come-- come out. Don't be afraid. Come into my house and I-- we'll talk inside. And I didn't answer. So she say it again. She was walking around and-- around and saying, so come out. Finally, I saw I have nothing else to do. And I walked out. And she took me into the house. And she said I will keep you there. And maybe I'll find some better place for you, or you stay with us. At night she let me stay in the house. In the daytime, I used to walk out from the house. She had the basement of potatoes. So I used to crawl in in those potatoes and hide there and lay a whole day in this basement, in the potatoes. The door was open that nobody shall see that somebody is there. They kept it specially open. So until it got real cold, she kept me there.

speaker: interviewer
text: And she was saying that one day somebody came, said, you know what? They will look for Jews here. It was called [NON-ENGLISH] this place. The place that you were staying?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: The village-- that was the name of the village?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know. There were a few houses there. But that was a-- [NON-ENGLISH] means water, river. So they called it behind the river. [NON-ENGLISH], it means. And I-- they told-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I can't remember.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you stayed there for how long?

speaker: interviewee
text: So when they said they will look for Jews, I was thinking-- I-- she was a little afraid. She said, I will bring you to my daughter in another village. And then I was thinking, she was so nice to me and so good to me. Maybe I'll try to go somewhere else. Maybe somebody will keep me or what, just for a little while, and then I'll come back to her. And she said, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] if you want, I was going to the farmer there, [NON-ENLIGH], because I knew them, and I thought, they will keep me there. But as I was going from there, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] and I was waiting it shall be dark. And I approached. I didn't even know that this man who brought me out food, that he is living there in that farm. And I was passing by his house. He was sitting by the window, because in the villages they want to save light, and they sit in the dark. I was waiting for the dark to pass by. But he was there, and he recognized me. And he ran out of the house. And he wanted-- he said, don't be afraid. Don't be afraid. Come-- come-- come in [NON-ENLIGH]. So I said-- I didn't-- I start running anyway, because I didn't know that it is him. And he said-- he was strong, because I wasn't-- walking-- not running. So he said, I am taking-- I want you to be here. There is another group. They were looking for you. I told them that there is somebody who survived from this group. And we want-- they want you to come there to see them.

speaker: interviewer
text: A group of partisans, this would be?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, this was other Jews who were hiding there. And I went with them to hiding, then. But being there [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] when-- I didn't want to go back to the forest. Even I knew there is a group, but I had no other choice. I figured-- he said he will keep me in his house until he'll get connected with them, because he was Jehovah's Witnesses, and those people were helping Jews-- Jehovah's Witnesses. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] He-- he used to-- to try to bring food to the other groups. And then they called over somebody from-- they let them know that I am by them. And they made arrangements to come for me. They came-- this Roman Biletsky and another one--

speaker: interviewer
text: These were the Jews came for you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. And one Jew. And one-- this Roman Biletsky is a Jehovah's Witness. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] They took me there to the forest. And when I saw this group, so I didn't want to leave no more. I wanted to stay with them. I was four-- four months still with them. We were hiding in a forest from pines. The pines are so low. And there, we had a bunker. And we were hiding there. In the wintertime, when it was snowing, we couldn't go out for the-- for the wood, or water. We used to scrub the snow and dissolve it. And in the bunker where we were was a small place. It was on one side higher, and the other side higher. It was like two beds made. We were 23 people, so 10 people were on this side, and 10 on this side. In the middle was like a kitchen. The two pails-- it was a hole-- two holes-- and we had a pail. And behind this, we could make an oven. It was like you could make a fire, and we cooked there at night. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Outside we had to see that the smoke shall not go out. We used to stay there and watch, always. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And when we were in there was a little place that three people could stand up. There wasn't place to turn. When we were laying and sleeping, nobody could lay flat. We were all only on the side. [CRYING] When we wanted to turn, everybody had to turn. And when we wanted to wash up, so one could wash one night, the other a night. And it was such-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] such a hard life there. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] We didn't have facilities to live, like we had lice and bugs. They were eating us alive. [SOBBING] [CRYING] But we continued to live like this. [SOBBING] We used to walk out at night. The boys to used to go and bring in-- those Jehovah's Witnesses helped us. They used to bring some food for 23 people. We needed something. We had, like, beans. We used to make soups.

speaker: interviewer
text: [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] So how long-- you were there till the end of the war?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: So tell me about what happened. How you got out. What happened? Someone liberated you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, the Russians came in. And we were liberated on March [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] 27th.

speaker: interviewee
text: '44. And then we walked out of there. We couldn't walk. We couldn't-- because we were mostly sitting, it was so hard. And we came to our city-- also we-- first, we had to go. We didn't know which way to go from there, but we came to our city, to Podhajce. And there was the front coming right away. We couldn't-- then we-- we couldn't stay there. We slept over one night.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did you sleep?

speaker: interviewee
text: Where? In the house, where Jewish people used to be on the floors. There was nothing there, and that nobody wanted to be. We all slept together on the floor. That's it. We didn't have what to put down, nothing. We were all in rags. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And we came to Monastyryska, from there to Buczacz. We were running with the front, together.

speaker: interviewer
text: With the Russians?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, the Russians liberated us. And there was, on the way, shooting. And when-- we were heading to Tarnow, then.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where was that, in Poland still?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And when we were walking south from-- from Buczacz, right away the Germans came. And it was a bridge. And we just passed the bridge.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they came, and they-- they broke the bridge. And you couldn't go no more. We just were saved by the minute.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we were walking there, to Tarnow, and the way was full of dead people from the front and animals, horses-- dead. But everybody was running from this group. And at one point, I was already so tired. I couldn't walk. And I didn't have any shoes. My feet were bleeding from-- it was sand and snow altogether.

speaker: interviewer
text: And I just-- who could ran a little better? Went, and I was left there.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I thought I cannot go any further no more. So then came a man, also.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.
